Introduction
Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a chronic, inflammatory, neurodegen-
erative disorder of the central nervous system (CNS) and the most 
common cause of nontraumatic disability in young adults.
143  
Approximately 1 million 203 individuals are affected with MS in 
the United States. It can present in numerous ways and affects 
several functional and cognitive systems. This patient population 
often develops challenges with gait, spasticity, cognition, fatigue, 
weakness, bladder and bowel function, and wounds. Physiatrists 
are well equipped to manage many of the challenges of MS that 
might otherwise go undetected or unacknowledged. An under -
standing of the pathogenetic, pharmacologic, and rehabilitative 
options for persons with MS (PwMS) is vital for the practicing 
physiatrist. The first disease-modifying therapy (DMT) for MS 
was approved in 1993. Since then, there has been rapid progress, 
so that there are currently 10 DMT options for PwMS. As the 
number of more and more effective DMTs for MS increases,  
patients and families continue to expect an improved quality of 
life and to hope for treatment options extending into optimal 
management of their many symptoms. Therefore the role of phys-
iatry for PwMS will continue to expand, and it is vital that the 
practitioner be familiar with the specificities of MS, what differ -
entiates it from other diseases, and the degree to which rehabilita-
tion can be of help to this population.
Costs
The estimated US annual cost for MS is $85.4 billion and a mean 
cost of $88,487 per patient per year, making it one of the costliest 
illnesses to treat.
100,140 Factors associated with increased per  
person-year direct costs include progressive MS vs. relapsing  
remitting, males vs. females, and those patients with comorbidi -
ties, including depression, paresthesia, fatigue, and gait disorders. 
These symptomatic areas are where physiatrists can have meaning-
ful and significant positive impact. Physical medicine and reha -
bilitation physicians are skilled at providing tools, resources, and 
interventions to mitigate many of these symptoms. The annual 
direct and indirect costs of MS per person are estimated to range 
from $8528 to $54,244.
1 Direct costs make up 77% of the total 
and are largely attributable to the cost of prescription medica -
tions.1 Contrary to the laws of supply and demand, each new 
DMT that is introduced has not driven market competition to 
lower prices. Although patients on DMT have higher direct costs 
than those not on DMT , outpatient visits cost more in the non-
DMT group. Studies have supported that DMT use has reduced 
the number of ED visits as well as less inpatient admissions. A key 
cost driver is progressive functional disability.
177 In lower levels of 
disability, the main cost drivers are DMT and direct medical care, 
whereas higher levels of disability are associated with direct non -
medical costs plus productivity losses.
PwMS, and an EDSS score below 4 is low minimal disability,  
6 through 8 moderate disability, and above 8 severe  
disability.
• Disease -modifying therapies should be offered to everyone 
with a diagnosis of MS with the goal of preventing relapses, 
reducing accumulation of neurologic impairment, and reducing 
neuronal injury; these are classified into monoclonal antibodies, 
oral therapies, and injectables.
• Childr en with MS tend to have a larger lesion burden, 
particularly with brainstem and cerebellar involvement, higher 
annualized relapse rate, and rapid recovery.
47
Multiple Sclerosis
SOPHIA KIERNAN, RAM NARENDRA NARAYAN, AND ANJALI N. SHAH
• T he most common disabling symptoms/challenges for patients 
with multiple sclerosis (PwMS) are gait, spasticity, cognitive 
changes, fatigue, weakness, bladder/bowel dysfunction, and 
wounds. These symptoms are well suited to management by a 
physiatrist.
• D espite the numerous advances in disease-modifying therapy, 
symptom management is a vital aspect of care for PwMS, and 
key aspects of management include fatigue mitigation; tone 
management through the use of oral, injectable, and 
intrathecal therapies; gait training; and judicious use of 
physical, occupational, and speech therapy.

used scoring system for assessing and monitoring disability in

Epidemiology
Approximately 2.3 million individuals are affected by MS world -
wide.86 Females are typically affected two to three times as often 
as males.114 Although females have a higher risk of having MS, 
those males who are affected tend to have a more aggressive 
course, more difficulty with recovery after an attack, and a more 
rapid development of disability.
25,44,45,179 The peak age of diagno-
sis is believed to be 20 to 40 years ( Fig. 47.1).127 Ethnicity has a 
role in disease progression and prognosis. Compared with Asian, 
Black, and Hispanic populations, the disease is more common in 
non-Hispanic Whites, with a lifetime incidence of approximately 
1 in 400.
43 It remains a disease that largely afflicts Whites with 
recent evidence showing greater disease severity in non-Whites.
MS is more common in Europe, the United States, Canada, 
New Zealand, and Australia and rare in the equatorial countries 
and on the Asian continent.
181,188 Migration studies have observed 
that individuals who move from high- to low-risk areas during 
adolescence or early childhood adopt the risk of the new area.  
The opposite has been shown to be true in those moving from 
low- to high-risk areas, but with less robustness.
5,68 In addition, 
birth month appears to be of importance in MS development. In 
a meta-analysis of 145,672 MS patients across various geographic 
locations and populations, it was found that MS births are higher

146 This was thought to result from low maternal sun 
exposure during pregnancy and possibly low antenatal levels of 
vitamin D.
Social determinants of health impact disease severity in Whites 
compared with Blacks because of limited access to medical care, 
specialists’ medical or rehabilitative interventions, and increased 
stress. Physicians should be aware of implicit bias when treating 
patients who may have varying cultural views of medical care. 
This is an area of increased awareness for PwMS and how it relates 
to different races and ethnicities.
207
Pathogenesis
Genetic Linkage
MS is thought to be an autoimmune disease in which immune and 
inflammatory cells attack the CNS, damaging myelin, axons, and 
neurons. Similar to many complex human diseases, MS develops 
in a genetically susceptible host who has experienced several envi-
ronmental triggers. This hypothesis is supported by the familial 
clustering of MS. Monozygotic twins have a 35% concordance 
rate, whereas dizygotic twins and first-degree relatives have a con-
cordance rate of approximately 4% if a family member or sibling 
has MS ( Table 47.1).
56,204 Also, serologic typing has found that 
human leukocyte antigen (HLA) is associated with the major his-
tocompatibility complex and various immune-mediated diseases; 
this observation has given rise to the field of immunogenetics. In 
MS, the HLA DRB1*1501 gene, located on chromosome 6p21, 
has been found to be strongly associated with the development of 
MS. The HLA genes have been the only consistent genetic linkage 
noted in MS. Heterozygous carriers have a three times and homo-
zygous carriers a six times increased risk of developing MS.
94,170 
The DR15 haplotype is also associated with narcolepsy and sys-
temic lupus erythematosus.
192,206
Environmental Factors
Environmental factors have been evaluated for their effect on the 
risk of developing MS as well as their influence on progression. 
The incidence of MS increases with distance from the equator.  
It has also been found that reduced levels of vitamin D increase 
the risk of MS development, particularly in the White popula -
tion.
7,138,139 A typical multivitamin contains approximately

400 International Units (IU) of vitamin D; 20 minutes of whole-
body sun exposure equates to approximately 10,000 IU of  
vitamin D. Further, research supports changing the minimum 
daily consumption of vitamin D because it is found to be protec-
tive in several other diseases. Studies are currently being con -
ducted to examine the influence of vitamin D levels on disease 
progression in MS.
7,138,139
A landmark study has convincingly established the role of 
childhood infection with Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) in the subse -
quent development of MS. Approximately 50% of children have 
EBV exposure by the age of 5 years, and approximately 80% to 
90% of the population is exposed by 20 years of age.
209 Primary 
EBV infection is typically asymptomatic in infants; it presents as 
infectious mononucleosis (IM) when reactivation occurs in ado -
lescents or young adults. Proponents of the EBV hypothesis have 
found that individuals exposed to late EBV and IM increase their 
risk of MS development by 2.3 times that of individuals exposed 
to EBV without IM. Never having been exposed to EBV reduced 
the risk of MS by one-tenth compared with individuals who had 
been exposed (Fig. 47.2).
6,122,193
Cigarette smoking is now a known risk factor for the develop-
ment of MS. 54,92,95,99,165 MS risk is approximately 50% higher  
in smokers and is associated with the intensity and duration of

adverse effect of smoking, the increasing ratio of female-male 
smokers has been proposed as an explanation for the increasing 
female-male ratio in MS incidence in several countries.
98 A cumu-
lative dose response exists between years and intensity of smoking 
and MS risk; cigarette smoking also appears to increase the rate of 
MS disease progression to secondary progressive MS (SPMS).
97,98 
The detrimental effects of smoking decline after 10 years of smok-
ing cessation despite the duration and intensity of abuse. Moist 
tobacco “snuffing” did not have the same effect or causation, lead-
ing researchers to study the irritating effect of tobacco smoke on 
the lungs and immune system as a possible cause.
93 Interestingly, 
alcohol consumption seems to protect a person from developing 
MS in a dose-dependent manner and attenuates the detrimental 
effects of smoking.
103
Other Factors
A strong correlation has been observed to exist between the body 
mass index (BMI) of females at 10 and 20 years of age. A BMI of 
over 20 increased the risk of MS, and a BMI 27 or above increased 
the risk by twofold.
72,96 Obese females between 10 and 20 years of 
age seem to be at greater risk than males during the same age range. 
An even stronger correlation of developing MS was demonstrated 
in females with a BMI 27 or above and HLA DRB1*1501 status.
72
Increased clinical and radiologic activity with increased  
sodium intake has been suggested as another cause of MS. This 
has been demonstrated in small-scale studies and is under further 
investigation.
58 Concerns about the risk of exposure to mercury, 
trace metals, organic solvents, or crude oil have not demonstrated 
convincing evidence.
130
Immunology
MS exhibits considerable heterogeneity in clinical presentation 
and disease course. It is considered to be an autoimmune disease 
by most experts, but what initiates the abnormal immune  
response is not clear. Most of the knowledge about the immuno -
pathogenesis of MS comes from the study of animal models, 
mainly experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE), in 
which immunization of an animal (e.g., a mouse or rat) with 
myelin components leads to a CD4 1 lymphocyte-orchestrated 
inflammatory response in the CNS. The pathologic hallmarks of 
acute MS lesions are perivenular immune cell infiltrate, demyelin-
ation, myelin-laden macrophages, edema, and axonal damage.
67 
Relapses in MS are thought to be mediated by peripherally acti -
vated helper lymphocytes that target the CNS.108 These autoreac-
tive lymphocytes may be activated through molecular mimicry, in 
which foreign antigens (e.g., viral or bacterial proteins) similar to 
CNS antigens activate the lymphocytes that will eventually react 
against the self-antigens. As an alternative explanation, antigens 
leaked from the CNS, probably owing to a previous unknown 
insult, can trigger the immune response by activating CD41 
helper T cells. These cells facilitate the recruitment and activation 
of other immune cells, which enter the brain and spinal cord  
by interacting with endothelial cells of the blood-brain barrier. 
Upon reactivation with autoantigens by CNS-resident antigen-
presenting cells, they damage myelin, axons, and neurons by vari-
ous effector mechanisms.
It is unclear which autoantigens ignite the autoimmune pro -
cess in MS, which is partly why the disease is so heterogeneous. 
In fact, as inflammation increases, access of the immune system to

previously compartmentalized antigens (i.e., epitope spreading) 
may occur, causing new epitopes in the same protein or other 
proteins to become new targets of the immune attack. 50
CD41  T cells, based on the cytokine milieu during their acti-
vation, can differentiate into functionally different effector cells 
and have been categorized into T-helper 1 (Th1), Th2, and Th17 
cells. It has been shown that both Th1 and Th17 cells are capable 
of inducing EAE in mice. Some of the MS therapies—including 
interferon (IFN)–beta, glatiramer acetate, and fingolimod—have 
been shown to dampen both Th1 and Th17 responses. 12,132,156  
In the context of autoimmune inflammatory demyelination,  
Th2 cell responses are considered to be protective and antiinflam-
matory, and several MS disease-modifying drugs are thought to 
shift the immune system toward the Th2 response.
Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) oligoclonal bands, produced by  
B-cell–derived plasma cells, are the most consistent immunologic 
abnormality in patients with MS. Also, B-cell depletion by mono-
clonal antibodies effectively decreases the inflammatory disease 
activity in MS (as manifested by new gadolinium-enhancing  
lesions on magnetic resonance imaging [MRI]). 90 These observa-
tions suggest a strong role for B cells in the pathogenesis of MS.
Inflammation and degeneration are present right from the 
earliest stages, and degeneration is thought to be the underlying 
cause of the irreversible and progressive disability that develops in 
most PwMS. 66 Neurodegeneration is directly or indirectly the 
result of inflammation and demyelination. Several studies have 
shown that the risk and the latency of entering the secondary 
progressive phase are not related to the number of exacerbations 
in the relapsing phase. 174,175 Measures of neurodegeneration,  
such as brain atrophy and cortical gray matter lesion load, are 
more strongly correlated with different measures of motor and 
cognitive disability.29,30 The pathogenic processes involved in pro-
gressive MS are quite complex and continue to pose a challenge 
in the discovery of therapeutic targets.
Subtypes
Clinically isolated syndrome (CIS) is defined as the first clinical 
episode that is consistent with a demyelinating etiology and is 
suggestive of MS. In several instances, CIS may be the first attack 
of relapsing remitting MS if the diagnostic criteria are fulfilled. 
Radiologically isolated syndrome is defined as the “incidental” 
identification of brain and spinal cord lesions on MRI that are 
highly suggestive of MS.35 In clinical practice, this situation usu -
ally occurs when MRI of the neuroaxis is obtained in a patient 
lacking symptoms and signs of MS (and when typically per -
formed for non-MS related reasons [headache, trauma, etc.]). 144 
Relapsing-remitting MS (RRMS) is the most common subtype 
and affects approximately 50% to 65% of PwMS. RRMS is char-
acterized by discrete relapses or “clinical attacks” consistent with 
demyelination with subsequent partial or full recovery and typi -
cally with no or only minimal progression during the interrelapse 
period, the minimum duration of which is arbitrarily fixed at  
1 month. SPMS becomes manifest when a patient with RRMS 
has a sustained deterioration of neurologic function (and resul -
tant accumulation of disability) with or without superimposed  
relapses. Usually, the relapse rate is very minimal in this phase. 
This subtype of MS ultimately develops in up to 90% of RRMS 
patients after 10 to 20 years of symptom onset. There is no  
biomarker that signifies the progression from RRMS to SPMS. 
Primary progressive MS (PPMS) affects males and females equally 
(1:1) and comprises approximately 10% to 15% of PwMS.  
This differs from RRMS in a noticeable lack of exacerbations; 
instead, there is an accumulation of disability over time. The 
speed of accumulation varies from person to person. Both SPMS 
and PPMS can further be characterized at different time points as 
either active (with relapses and/or new MRI activity) or not active 
and with progression (evidence of sustained disability progression 
over a specific time period as measured by the EDSS) or without  
progression.
Diagnosis
Clinical Assessment
The underlying basis for the diagnosis of MS is focal or multifocal 
demyelination involving the CNS occurring at different points of 
time. Therefore, as explained later, dissemination in space (DIS) 
and dissemination in time (DIT) criteria are applied for any 
clinical or radiologic presentation that is consistent with CNS 
demyelination. A typical patient is a young adult with one or 
several clinically distinct episodes or “attacks” of CNS dysfunc -
tion. Examples of such clinical episodes/attacks characteristic of 
MS include optic neuritis, long tract symptoms/signs (extremity 
weakness or paresthesias, spasticity, gait difficulty, sphincter dys -
function, brisk and pathologic deep tendon reflexes, presence of 
Babinski sign), brainstem dysfunction (diplopia, vertigo, facial 
weakness), cerebellar dysfunction (slurred speech, cerebellar nys -
tagmus, gait ataxia), among others. Symptoms and signs associ -
ated with a relapse typically extend beyond a 24-hour period and 
last anywhere between days and weeks. The time interval between 
two distinct relapses is arbitrarily fixed at 1 month. Clinical clues 
that are highly suggestive of MS include relapses in which symp -
toms usually develop acutely (over a few hours) or subacutely 
(over a few days) and remissions (partial or complete resolution) 
over the ensuing weeks or months, Lhermitte sign, internuclear 
ophthalmoplegia, fatigue (see later), and heat sensitivity (Uhthoff  
phenomenon). Clinical features that are atypical of MS are steady 
progression of symptoms (except with the PPMS variant), onset 
before 10 or after 50 years, predominantly or only cortical deficits 
(e.g., aphasia, neglect, apraxia, neglect, dementia-like presenta -
tion, convulsions), and hyperacute onset of symptoms (within 
minutes). As mentioned earlier, MS may rarely also present as 
overwhelming fatigue or generalized weakness in certain patients. 
This symptom may be misconstrued as fatigue caused by other 
nonmedical factors (busy lifestyles or excessive demands because 
of family or work obligations) or other metabolic, endocrine, or 
nonneurologic causes (Boxes 47.1 and 47.2).
Neuroimaging
In addition to clinical symptoms, MRI is recommended for the 
diagnostic evaluation of MS. Radiologic imaging can supplement 
or support clinical and laboratory data. The International Panel  
on Diagnosis of MS (the Panel) recommends screening with MR  
sequencing. The McDonald criteria are the most current and 
widely used to diagnose MS ( Table 47.2). These criteria went 
through updates in 2001, 2005, and 2010; the most recent version 
was released in 2017. 131,154,155,194 What has remained consistent 
throughout these updates is that MS can be diagnosed based on 
clinical symptoms alone. T wo clinical attacks involving different 
brain areas at two separate points (separated by at least 1 month) 
in time fulfill the criteria required to diagnose a PwMS. The crite-
rion for DIS on MRI is met by having one or more T2 lesions in

two of five locations in the CNS: periventricular, cortical, juxtacor-
tical, infratentorial, and spinal cord ( Box 47.3). DIT on MRI is 
met by the simultaneous presence of asymptomatic or symptom -
atic gadolinium-enhancing lesions and nonenhancing lesions or 
new T2 and/or a gadolinium-enhancing lesion or lesions on a 
follow-up MRI (Box 47.4). It should also be noted that although 
CSF analysis for the presence of CSF-specific oligoclonal bands is 
not mandatory to make the diagnosis of MS, it may be used to 
meet DIT criteria when such criteria is not met clinically or radio-
logically. The role of CSF analysis is further detailed later.65
When making a diagnosis of MS with the 2017 McDonald 
criteria, it should be kept in mind that there should be no better 
explanation for the clinical presentation. This is because certain 
immune-mediated and infectious conditions resulting in CNS  
demyelination can mimic MS and can meet the McDonald criteria. 
These include neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorders (NMOSD), 
acute transverse myelitis, acute disseminated encephalomyelitis 
(ADEM), myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein–associated disease 
(MOGAD), Lyme disease, among others. The 2015 diagnostic 
criteria for NMOSD include separate criteria in the presence and 
absence of the aquaporin-4 antibody. Differentiation of NMOSD 
from MS is vital because treatment is distinct for each disorder. 
Acute transverse myelitis presents acutely, may be monophasic or 
multiphasic, and has a clearly defined sensory level with exclusion 
of other causes. It is a focal inflammatory disorder of the spinal cord 
and can result in abnormalities in motor, sensory, and/or auto -
nomic function.196 The MOGAD syndrome has also emerged as a 
mimic of MS and NMOSD. It tends commonly to present as 
longitudinally extensive, frequently bilateral optic neuritis (usually 
with excellent recovery), long-segment transverse myelitis, and  
relapsing ADEM, although rarer and atypical presentations have 
been reported. Current treatment guidelines are evolving but are 
largely similar to those for NMOSD.195
In addition to its usefulness in diagnostic decision making, 
MRI is frequently used in making clinical decisions regarding 
the effectiveness of DMTs ( Fig. 47.3). MRI can reflect subclini -
cal and preclinical changes and is useful in monitoring any  
underlying disease activity. 119 Suggested MRIs for diagnostic 
and clinical decision making include T1 images with and with -
out contrast, T2 images, and fluid-attenuated inversion recovery 
(FLAIR) sequencing. T1 images with and without contrast  
determine the presence of active lesions. Areas of breakdown in 
the blood-brain barrier, characterized by an inflammatory defect 
or disruption, result in perivascular changes and allow uptake of 
gadolinium. Such lesions subsequently enhance on T1 images. 
Contrast-enhancing lesions will remain visible for approximately 
4 to 12 weeks, are associated with new or active lesions, and are 
indicative of clinically active disease. 82,111 This observation is  
often used as a marker for disease control in both clinical and 
research settings. Key technical factors include waiting approxi -
mately 15 minutes after contrast infusion to ensure accurate 
imaging. Gadolinium-enhancing lesions have been weakly cor -
related with disability or the accumulation of impairment. 107,119
Black hole lesions are hypointense, nonenhancing T1 lesions. 
They are correlated with chronic axonal damage and loss on MR 
sequences as well as on histopathologic examination. 119,200,201 
Their presence is associated with worsening cognitive function. 
Widening of the third ventricle is also associated with cortical 
atrophy and is again correlated with cognitive dysfunction. 19 
Reduction in T1 black hole formation is used as a biomarker for 
neuroprotection.
FLAIR imaging suppresses the CSF hyperintense signal in  
T2 images and allows for clearer demarcation of hyperintensities 
associated with the MS plaques. The short T1 inversion recovery 
(STIR) sequence is a relatively new technique used to better visu-
alize the spinal cord. Plaques and hyperintensities are viewed sig -
nificantly better with STIR imaging than previous T2 or proton 
density views (Fig. 47.4).141 Imaging frequency is often a clinician 
preference. New T2 or FLAIR lesions as well as active lesions help 
to determine the efficacy of DMT and correlate with the long-
term accumulation of disability. Although MRI is often helpful in 
the diagnostic workup process, it is not required for the diagnosis. 
As stated previously, clinical criteria alone can suffice. In the set -
ting of a patient with no abnormal or atypical MRI findings, CSF 
analysis and evoked potential studies may be needed for addi -
tional support.

aAn attack (relapse or exacerbation) is defined as patient-reported or objectively observed events typical of an acute inflammatory demyelinating event in the CNS, current or historical, with duration of 
at least 24 hours, in the absence of fever or infection. It should be documented by contemporaneous neurologic examination, but some historical events with symptoms and evolution characteristic for 
MS but for which no objective neurologic findings are documented can provide reasonable evidence of a previous demyelinating event. Reports of paroxysmal symptoms (historical or current) should, 
however, consist of multiple episodes occurring over not less than 24 hours. Before a definite diagnosis of MS can be made, at least 1 attack must be corroborated by findings on neurologic 
examination, visual evoked potential response in patients reporting previous visual disturbance, or MRI consistent with demyelination in the area of the CNS implicated in the historical report of 
neurologic symptoms.
bA clinical diagnosis based on objective clinical findings for 2 attacks is most secure. Reasonable historical evidence for 1 past attack in the absence of documented objective neurologic findings can 
include historical events with symptoms and evolution characteristics for a previous inflammatory demyelinating event; at least 1 attack, however, must be supported by objective findings.
cNo additional tests required. However, it is desirable that any diagnosis of MS be made with access to imaging based on these criteria. If imaging or other tests (e.g., CSF) are undertaken and are 
negative, extreme caution must be taken before making a diagnosis of MS and alternative diagnoses must be considered. There must be no better explanation for the clinical presentation, and 
objective evidence must be present to support a diagnosis of MS.
dGadolinium-enhancing lesions are not required; symptomatic lesions are excluded from consideration in individuals with brainstem or spinal cord syndromes.

Electrophysiologic Assessment
Neurophysiologic testing, such as visual, somatosensory, and brain-
stem auditory evoked potentials, may also be helpful in cases where 
clinical, radiologic, or CSF testing is inconclusive. Evoked potential 
testing is noninvasive and detects abnormalities throughout the 
length of the sensory pathway. This is helpful in cases where  
patients may have clinical symptoms of optic neuritis, including 
periorbital pain, photosensitivity, or visual changes. A prolongation 
in the p100 pathway is considered abnormal. Occasionally, a pro-
longation in the p100 pathway of the unaffected eye may also be 
seen.
116 Somatosensory evoked potentials detect delays along the 
median and tibial motor pathways. Brainstem auditory evoked 
potentials detect delays along the auditory pathway. There is some 
evidence that combining visual evoked potential and somatosen -
sory evoked potential testing significantly increases the diagnostic 
yield.
197 Currently, only visual evoked potentials are included in the 
MS diagnostic guidelines.154

Disability Classification
The EDSS is an ordinal clinical rating scale ranging from 0  
(no impairment) to 10 (death) (Fig. 47.5). It is based on a detailed 
neurologic examination and includes functional systems evaluat-
ing pyramidal, cerebellar, brainstem, sensory, bowel and bladder, 
visual, cerebral/mental, and miscellaneous functions. An EDSS 
score of 4 or less implies minimal disability with no ambulation 
restriction; a score of 6 to 8 signifies ambulation with the use of 
an assistive device and progressing as score increases to nearly 
nonambulatory; and a score of 8 or more identifies a person who 
is essentially nonambulatory or who is restricted to bed. The 
EDSS is one of the most widely used MS scales clinically and for 
research purposes. From a functional perspective, Kurtzke should 
be credited for his emphasis on disability measurement and its 
correlation to other systems at such an early time.
111,112
Pharmacologic Management
Disease-Modifying Therapies
DMTs should be offered to everyone with a diagnosis of MS,  
especially in the setting of RRMS. The main goals of DMT ini -
tiation are preventing relapses, reducing accumulation of neuro -
logic impairment and disability, and reducing neuronal injury.  
It is highly recommended to start DMTs as soon as possible to 
optimize these goals.
158
Classification of Disease-Modifying Therapies
A popular method of classifying DMTs for MS is by the method 
of administration: infusion, oral, and injectable drugs.
• M onoclonal antibodies. These include intravenous medications, 
such as natalizumab, ocrelizumab, rituximab, ublituximab, and 
alemtuzumab, and the subcutaneous injectable medication 
ofatumumab. These DMTs have high efficacy and are typically 
preferred for patients with more active disease. However, several 
of these medications are associated with serious adverse effects, 
including infections.
• O ral therapies. Oral medications such as fumarates (dimethyl 
fumarate, diroximel fumarate, monomethyl fumarate), sphin-
gosine-1-phosphate receptor (S1PR) modulators (fingolimod, 
siponimod, ozanimod, ponesimod), teriflunomide, and cladrib-
ine have varying efficacy and adverse effect profiles. In general, 
the fumarates are considered to have moderate efficacy, S1PR 
modulators are considered to have moderate to high efficacy, 
and teriflunomide is considered to have low efficacy (similar to 
platform injectable therapies). An exception, cladribine, is con-
sidered to be a high-efficacy, cell-depleting therapy.
• P latform injectable therapies. These include beta-1b and beta-
1a interferons and glatiramer acetate. These medications are 
considered to have low efficacy and injection-related adverse 
effects but a more favorable safety profile. These medications 
are falling out of favor in the current treatment landscape of 
MS given the rapidly expanding armamentarium of DMT .
The main factor in choosing a DMT (especially a high-efficacy 
DMT) for a patient is the presence of features associated with 
poor prognosis and higher risk of future relapse and disability. 
These include demographic factors such as onset after 30 years; 
male sex; Black, Asian, or Hispanic ethnicity; clinical features 
such as motor or cerebellar symptoms at disease onset; poor recov-
ery from a relapse; frequent early relapses; radiologic features such 
as high burden of T2 lesion volume on MRI, enhancing or new 
T2 lesions on MRI, and the presence of spinal cord lesions; and 
the use of prior DMTs. Of note, most of the studies examining 
prognostic factors have not examined all factors in representative 
populations. For instance, ethnic minorities are largely underrep-
resented in these studies, which predominately tend to enroll 
White patients of European ancestry.
26
With regard to selecting the initial DMT for patients, there 
are two broad treatment paradigms: starting with a highly effec -
tive therapy (early aggressive approach) or starting with low-
efficacy, low-risk therapy (gradual escalation approach).
87 In the 
early aggressive approach, one of the high-efficacy DMTs (e.g., 
natalizumab, ocrelizumab, ofatumumab, cladribine) is used as 
the initial DMT . The main advantage is the initial aggressive 
control of disease activity (relapse rate reduction and develop -
ment of new MRI changes), which is maximum in the initial 
years of diagnosis per natural history studies and plays a major 
role in long-term disability accumulation. In the early phase, 
patients tend to be relatively younger and tend to tolerate 
DMTs better. The main con is the risk of severe adverse effects 
(e.g., serious infections, progressive multifocal leukoencepha -
lopathy). With regard to the gradual escalation approach,  
the initial DMT is typically a low- or moderate-efficacy DMT 
like one of the platform therapies or fumarates. Escalation to  
a high-efficacy DMT is then implemented when the patient 
develops new MS relapses or new MRI activity. Although  
immunosuppressive risk mitigation is argued to be the main 
advantage in this approach, the main disadvantage is the  
rapid accumulation of disability in the early phase of disease.

Rehabilitation, Exercise, and  
Symptom Management
Physical Activity
A person with a disability is prone to becoming inactive and  
deconditioned.166 The disabled population was often previously 
overlooked in regard to large-scale recommendations for exercise. 
PwMS were previously advised to not exercise for fear of worsen -
ing their disease course or out of concern for the effects of over -
heating. Research has led to the contrary: Immune profile studies 
have demonstrated no change in the physiologic variables of  
patients with MS compared with control groups after being sub -
jected to 30 minutes of aerobic exercise.
75 Additionally, meta  
review and systematic analysis have shown no increase in relapse 
or adverse events for exercise training as compared with a control 
group.
120 Indeed, multiple studies have demonstrated the safety 
and benefit of exercise in PwMS regardless of MS subtype in 
terms of aerobic fitness, quality of life, and overall health, with 
greatest benefit being shown in physical health-related quality of 
life.
48,149 Advice on exercise and physical fitness was most com -
monly sought by individuals with MS, even more commonly  
requested than information on medications.186
The first and only consensus panel was convened to review the 
available evidence and formulate guidelines for physical fitness  
in PwMS.118 The review determined that there was sufficient evi-
dence to formulate guidelines to improve aerobic capacity and

quality of life benefits ( Table 47.4). These recommendations  
are based on the recommendations for physical activity of the  
MS Society of Canada.
140
PwMS commonly report fatigue, mood disorders, inadequate 
access to equipment, heat intolerance, and insufficient time as 
barriers to exercising.
4 Clinicians should continue to reinforce the 
importance of fitness and make efforts to remove or treat those 
barriers when possible. In summary, exercise improves quality of 
life in PwMS, with no evidence of deleterious effects. The inten -
sity, duration, and frequency of exercise must be considered  
in relation to each patient’s symptoms, heat intolerance, fall risk, 
strength, and endurance.
Gait Impairment
Mobility impairment is among the most common single disabili -
ties in PwMS. Approximately 75% of PwMS have mobility chal -
lenges.167 Natural history studies conducted before the availability 
of DMTs reported that 50% of PwMS who had the onset of gait 
impairment would require an assistive device 15 years after their 
diagnosis.
205 Physiatrists are keenly aware of the detrimental  
effects of mobility impairments in the disabled. PwMS experience 
an increased risk of weakness, spasticity, contractures, bone min-
eral changes, cardiovascular changes, reduced independence, and 
reduced quality of life. Walking is more challenging for patients

who have spasticity-related symptoms. PwMS have a decrease in 
walking speed and step length, an increase in step width, and a 
decrease in hip extension during the stance phase; they have a 
decrease in knee flexion during the swing period; and a decrease 
in ankle dorsiflexion in initial contact and a decrease in ankle 
plantarflexion during the preswing phase.41 The most discrimina-
tive individual element of gait difference between PwMS and a 
healthy control population is decreased knee range of motion. 61 
Overall, long-term positive prognostic indicators for disability 
and gait impairment include female sex, young age, complete  
recovery of initial exacerbation (specific to the RRMS subtype), 
and reduced relapse rate in the first 5 years. 198
The Timed 25-Foot Walk (T25FW) test is a validated measure 
of walking speed in MS. A 20% change in walking speed is consid-
ered significant and clinically meaningful.110 A time of 5 seconds or 
less is expected in unaffected individuals. Other gait tests, such as 
the 2- and 6-minute walk tests (6MWT), have demonstrated cor-
relation to ambulation and physical fatigue in PwMS.50,74,77 These 
tests take longer to administer, but additional information, such  
as fatigue resistance or sensitivity, speed, heart rate monitoring, and 
endurance, may be obtained. 50 However, the greater output of  
energy required, combined with the possibility that some patients 
may not be able to complete either test, makes the T25FW test 
more feasible in a clinical and research setting.
Dalfampridine is the extended-release form of 4-aminopyridine, 
a potassium channel blocker that binds to open potassium channels 
in the CNS, resulting in enhanced nerve signal transmission and 
prolonged mechanism of action. Dalfampridine is particularly  
appealing for treatment of MS because of its ability to enhance 
nerve conduction in areas of demyelination.153 Dalfampridine was 
approved for use in PwMS with walking impairment based on the 
results of two phase 3 randomized placebo-controlled trials. A 2021 
meta-analysis reported that dalfampridine improved both short 
(measured with T25FW) and long (measured with 6MWT) walk-
ing distances, as well as hand dexterity and mental function. 212 
Dalfampridine is given at a dose of 10 mg every 12 hours. A history 
of seizures is an absolute contraindication because dalfampridine 
lowers the seizure threshold. Other adverse events include increased 
frequency of urinary tract infections, vertigo, insomnia, headache, 
and falls. Dalfampridine is renally excreted; therefore a baseline 
basic metabolic panel is advised to confirm adequate creatinine 
clearance (. 50 mL/min).59,79–81
Only 30% to 40% of patients are dalfampridine responders, so 
it is reasonable to trial the medication and discontinue it if the 
patient is not seeing benefit. Various theories explaining respond-
ers vs. nonresponders include varying sites of demyelination.
Fatigue
Fatigue remains one of the most common symptoms of MS. This 
is often quoted as the single most disabling symptoms. Patients 
may have difficulty comprehending and articulating the nature of 
their fatigue; they face challenges in explaining to others why “they 
look so good” but may feel entirely the contrary. Patients com -
monly describe fatigue as a “reversible motor and cognitive impair-
ment with reduced motivation and desire to rest.” 135 Individuals 
with MS may experience two types of fatigue: peripheral and 
central. Peripheral fatigue is associated with fatigability (i.e., a 
generalized sense of exhaustion after a few minutes of physical  
activity that is alleviated with rest). Central fatigue is subjective 
and is associated with dysfunction in arousal and attention.  
The individual reports a feeling of constant exhaustion or lassitude 
that can lead to worsening vision and/or function, as well as  
significant impact on function, quality of life, relationships, and 
maintenance of occupation/vocation. Rest does not influence 
central fatigue.
The Fatigue Severity Scale, the Fatigue Impact Scale, and the 
Modified Fatigue Impact Scale are the most commonly used self-
reported scales for fatigue assessment in PwMS. 63,135 The Modi-
fied Fatigue Impact Scale was modified from the Fatigue Impact 
Scale and was created for clinical settings; it takes 5 to 10 minutes 
to complete. Long (21 questions) and short (5 questions) ver -
sions are available.117 Fatigue can also be classified as primary and 
secondary. Primary fatigue is attributable to the disease process 
(MS), and secondary fatigue is a result of other causes. Screening 
for secondary causes of fatigue—including metabolic, endocrine, 
hematologic, and medication side effect profiles—is advised  
(Box 47.5). Considering there is up to a 50% lifetime prevalence 
of depression in PwMS, screening for depression is advised  
because mood disturbances may mimic fatigue or lassitude. 39,180 
The treatment of depression in PwMS is associated with  
improvement of fatigue symptoms.
Aggressive screening for and treatment of fatigue are advised. 
Awareness of triggering factors, such as heat, stress, or overexer -
tion, should be emphasized, and energy conservation techniques 
and training are advised. Education of family and caregivers is 
helpful because MS fatigue may present suddenly. In addition to 
energy conservation and avoidance of triggers, pharmacologic 
treatment is advised to help maintain energy and focus. Patients 
respond differently to these medications, and the clinician is  
encouraged to try another agent if the patient experiences a sub -
therapeutic response to one of them ( Table 47.5).
Sleep Disorders
Approximately 10% of the general population suffers from insom-
nia, compared with approximately 40% to 50% of PwMS who 
report difficulty with sleep initiation/maintenance or have early-
morning awakening.125 Any discussion about fatigue in PwMS 
should first include a discussion about sleep hygiene. The clinician 
should encourage sleep-promoting behaviors, such as avoiding eat-
ing, drinking, watching television, or even reading in bed. Specific 
symptoms of MS that may interfere with sleep include spasticity, 
pain/paresthesias, and nocturia.152 Reviewing side effects of medi-
cations and DMTs, particularly IFNs, may reveal myalgias or low-
grade fever on injection days that cause sleep disruption. Finally, 
screening for mood disorders is advised. It is vitally important

to address and adequately treat these symptoms. Patients have  
demonstrated insomnia benefit with guided imagery, biofeedback, 
cognitive behavioral therapy, and meditation practice. If pharma-
cologic treatment is required, a determination of long- or short-
term use is needed. T reatment agents include zolpidem, trazodone, 
benzodiazepine, sedating antidepressants, and antihistamines.
32
Sleep-disordered breathing, obstructive sleep apnea, and central 
sleep apnea may be the etiology of persistent fatigue in PwMS. 
Obstructive sleep apnea is a result of upper airway obstruction 
during sleep, and central sleep apnea is the lack of respiratory  
effort. Patients with clinical or radiologic evidence of brainstem 
involvement were found to have a higher apnea-hypopnea index 
(episodes of obstructive apneas, central apneas, or hypopneas per 
hour of sleep) than those without brainstem involvement.
21,125 
There is a suggestion that DMT use results in lower apnea 
scores.
21,152 Referral for polysomnography testing is advised in the 
patient who is not helped by fatigue-mitigating treatment. T reat-
ment may include weight loss, tobacco cessation, respiratory sup-
port with continuous positive airway pressure, or surgery.
Besides obstructive sleep apnea and central sleep apnea, other 
sleep-related movement disorders include restless leg syndrome 
(RLS) and periodic limb movement disorder (PLMD). Patients 
with RLS typically report an inability to keep their legs still. 
PLMD typically manifests while sleeping and involves involun-
tary movements, most commonly in the legs. PLMD is quantified 
and confirmed via polysomnography testing. An increased risk for 
RLS is seen with older age, increased disability, cervical lesions, 
and PPMS. Patients with RLS should undergo screening for iron 
deficiency because this may contribute to symptoms. T reatment 
for both RLS and PLMD includes dopaminergic agents, benzodi-
azepine (clonazepam), and anticonvulsants.
106
Narcolepsy is a chronic sleep disorder characterized by sleep 
paralysis, cataplexy, hypnagogic hallucinations, and nocturnal 
sleep disruption.
118 MS is the fourth most common cause of nar -
colepsy. The same gene is responsible for both MS and narcolepsy 
(HLA DRB1*1501), suggesting a similar autoimmune compo -
nent to narcolepsy.
Mood Disorders
The most common mood disorder in MS is depression. It is pres-
ent in at least 50% of patients and presents the hypotheses that 
comorbid depression develops when facing a chronic illness and 
that there is a disease-specific process related to the CNS that 
predisposes PwMS to depression.
159 However, PwMS have more 
episodes of severe depression than patients with other chronic 
neurologic disorders.
157 The lifetime prevalence of depression in 
MS is three times that of the general population; however, depres-
sive disorders in MS tend to have predominant anxiety, irritabil -
ity, and anger. The overlap of symptoms in both depression and 
MS (fatigue, poor concentration, sleepiness/fatigue, and appetite 
disturbance) can lead to underdiagnosis of depression and/or  
delay the diagnosis of MS. The suicide rate of patients with MS is 
7.5 times higher than that of the general population and higher 
than that in people with other neurologic disorders.
171
Screening for DMT-related side effects, particularly IFNs, is 
advised because some patients may develop mood disorders as a  
side effect of DMT . The Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) and the

Beck Depression Inventory–Fast Screen (BDI-FS) are validated 
scales to screen for depression in PwMS. 17,190 Both are patient-
reported scales; the BDI has 21 items, and the BDI-FS has 7 items. 
The BDI has a sensitivity of 71% and a specificity of 79%. The high 
false-negative rate (30%) should be noted; those patients who are 
borderline may need further testing or more frequent evaluations. 
Although limited studies are available regarding controlled studies 
of pharmacologic treatment in depression, the Goldman Consensus 
Group, an expert panel convened by the National MS Society to 
review depression in MS, recommends treating depression with 
individualized treatment using medication(s) and/or psychological 
counseling.76 The limited data available support the use of selective 
serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) or duloxetine.159 Addition-
ally, a new area of focus is reviewing one’s coping skills for living an 
enriching, fulfilling life despite one’s disability.
Bipolar disorder is also more prevalent in PwMS than in the 
general population, most commonly bipolar II.33 Clinicians must 
be careful to also ask about manic or hypomanic episodes when 
screening patients for depression.
Similar to depression, anxiety is approximately three times 
more common in PwMS than in the general population. 40 The 
most common anxiety disorder is general anxiety disorder,  
followed by panic disorder. Anxiety in MS may be instigated  
by development of the disease, decline in functional abilities,  
and fear of disease progression and/or disability. T reatment is 
similar to anxiety in the general population and includes SSRIs/  
serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors, buspirone, psycho-
therapy, and cognitive behavioral therapy.
Pseudobulbar affect (PBA) is known to affect PwMS. Please 
see the specific section addressing this topic later in the chapter.
Thermoregulation
The pathophysiology of MS centers around central demyelin -
ation.67 Physiatrists are aware of the detrimental effects of segmental 
demyelination: reduction in conduction velocity, altered saltatory 
conduction, and eventual conduction block. The safety factor (ratio 
of action current available at a node to threshold current) is respon-
sible for successful conduction. Advanced demyelination reduces 
the safety factor. Heat causes alteration of the sodium channels, 
reduces the safety factor, and decreases the amplitude of nerve  
action potentials. A demyelinated nerve is more vulnerable to heat-
related changes than an unaffected nerve.113
Heat intolerance is a well-established symptom in PwMS. The 
Uhthoff phenomenon, a transient worsening of neurologic func -
tion (initially described as blurry vision after a hot bath), with 
heat exposure, physical exhaustion (exercise), infection, or dehy -
dration is very common in PwMS. 53 Heat causes transient wors -
ening of many symptoms of MS and can cause fatigue. The “hot 
bath test” was used before the availability of MRI to diagnose 
patients with MS.129 Patients were immersed in warm water rang-
ing from 35°C to 40°C and observed for worsening of long tract 
corticobulbar and corticospinal signs. This was discontinued after 
some patients had irreversible changes after heating, as well as 
after the advent of more accurate diagnosis with MRI.84 Approxi-
mately 58% to 93% of patients are affected by heat-induced  
fatigue.24 Exposure to heat can cause worsening of both physical 
and cognitive function. Autonomic dysfunction also contributes 
to poor temperature regulation in PwMS.53
Patients should be counseled about prevention and treatment 
options to avoid the occurrence of potentially fatal loss of motor 
control leading to drowning. Avoidance of warm baths or swimming 
in heated pools is advised. In general, water temperatures no greater 
than 84°F (26.7°C to 28.9°C) are advised.172 Precooling by drinking 
ice water or taking a cool water bath can effectively lower the core 
body temperature. Head cooling compared with simulated, sham 
cooling has also been found to be effective in PwMS.164 In a com-
parison study, PwMS reported more discomfort and physical/men-
tal fatigue during heat and cold exposure as compared with healthy 
controls, which could also contribute to general temperature intoler-
ance.38 Contrary to early advice that theorized exercise may induce 
heat intolerance, PwMS are now encouraged to exercise in modera-
tion for strength, fitness, and maintenance of health. Many evapora-
tive cooling garments are available to help patients when they are 
outside and at risk of overheating.178
Spasticity
Spasticity affects up to 85% of PwMS.167 Lower limb spasticity is 
much more common (97%) than upper limb spasticity (50%). 10 
Unsurprisingly, there is a negative correlation between the pres -
ence of lower limb spasticity and mobility.187 The Consortium of 
MS Centers recommends that spasticity be screened for as part of 
routine MS patient visits. In addition to evaluating a patient’s 
strength, range of motion, deep tendon reflexes, and muscle tone 
with the patient seated on the examination table, the patient 
should be examined in a dynamic setting. This includes observing 
the patient’s gait pattern, observing how the patient’s intrinsic 
hand muscles move with flexion and extension, and observing for 
signs of muscle cocontraction. Patients should also be asked about 
difficulty falling asleep caused by increased spasticity at night.
Many tools are available to assess spasticity. These include the 
Ashworth, Modified Ashworth, and Tardieu tests; the Penn Spasm 
Frequency Scale; and the Timed Up and Go T est. In addition, the 
T25FW test, the Ambulation Index, and the MS Spasticity Scale 
are MS-specific tests that can be used in the clinical setting. 89,101 
The MS Spasticity Scale has been validated for use in PwMS and 
is an 88-item self-reported scale.
T reatment options for spasticity are discussed in further detail 
in Chapter 24. A brief overview is presented here. Both physical 
and occupational therapy are helpful in early stages and as adjunc-
tive treatment to supplement oral, injected, and intrathecal medi-
cations. Oral baclofen is the usual first-line oral agent. Tizanidine 
may be of limited use given its sedating side effects. Clonazepam is 
helpful in cases of PLMD or nighttime spasms. According to a 
2022 Cochrane review, cannabis products have good evidence for 
improving spasticity and measurements of well-being with only 
slight increases in discontinuation of therapy caused by side effects 
as compared with placebo.60 The American Academy of Neurology 
released a guideline stating that oral cannabis is “the only comple-
mentary and alternative medicine unequivocally effective for help-
ing patients with MS, specifically easing their pain and symptoms 
of spasticity, possibly for as long as 1 year of treatment.” The con-
sensus statement reported on the beneficial effects of oral cannabis 
in the treatment of spasticity, pain, muscle spasms, and poor sleep. 
However, the Cochrane review only included a small trial that 
compared cannabis and placebo for effect on neuropathic pain, 
and the study showed low certainty evidence.60
The benefit of chemodenervation with botulinum toxin 
(BoNT) injections in MS is paramount; its use results from the 
difficulty in tolerating oral antispasmodics because of sedation or 
fatigue. BoNT therapy can be augmented using add-on extracor -
poreal shock wave therapy, which is a series of sonic pulses that 
works to reduce tone in spastic limbs. 121,140 Cryoneurolysis

Intrathecal baclofen (ITB) therapy via an implanted drug deliv-
ery device is indicated when oral or injectable treatment becomes 
ineffective. The clinician is encouraged to initiate therapy before 
the onset of significant walking impairment or lower limb weak -
ness. The use of ITB pumps in patients with MS has demonstrated 
improvements in spasticity, quality of life, sleep quality, bowel  
and bladder performance, skin integrity, and ambulation with  
low complication rates in numerous studies. 14,148 Physiatrists are  
encouraged to educate patients about ITB therapy and to provide 
it as a treatment option for PwMS. Studies have demonstrated that 
ITB therapy in PwMS is not introduced as a result of a greater 
focus on DMT management. Also, the patient or clinician may 
not fully recognize the effect of spasticity on quality of life and may 
not know how to manage ITB therapy, hence the need for the 
physiatrist, who will be better able to recognize and effectively treat 
and manage spasticity.57
The growing complexity and availability of DMTs for MS has 
reduced the time that many MS specialists give to the treatment 
of symptoms. The growing complexity and availability of DMTs 
for MS has resulted in many specialists referring to physiatry for 
the treatment of gait and spasticity problems. The preservation of 
clinically useful mobility and overall function is most important 
to the PwMS. A multimodal and multidisciplinary approach is 
vital in approaching the patient with a chronic, progressive disease 
such as MS.
Pain
Pain in PwMS can be divided into neuropathic pain, pain associ-
ated with DMT injection, trigeminal neuralgia, optic neuritis,  
a Lhermitte sign, and the “MS hug.” Patients with MS experi -
ence both neuropathic and nociceptive pain. Prevalence studies 
report that 29% to 86% of patients are affected by pain. 183,184 
The Neuropathic Pain Special Interest Group of the International 
Association for the Study of Pain defines neuropathic pain arising 
as a direct consequence of a lesion or disease that affects the  
somatosensory system.70 Nociceptive pain is attributable to stim -
ulation of nociceptors that signal tissue irritation or injury to  
elicit an appropriate response. Pain has a negative effect on mood,  
energy, daily activities, vocation, and social interaction and has 
been linked with reduced quality of life in PwMS.
Neuropathic pain has a prevalence of 50% in MS and is  
believed to be a result of the presence of plaques in the CNS 
(particularly the corticospinal and dorsal column tracts), causing 
symptoms of allodynia and hyperalgesia. 185 Patients respond well 
to anticonvulsant treatment, particularly gabapentin, which has 
little drug interaction and a wide dose range. Another agent in the 
same class is pregabalin. Although effective for its treatment ben -
efit, pregabalin’s side effects (edema, sedation, weight gain, consti-
pation) are difficult for some patients to tolerate. Finally, other 
agents in the anticonvulsant class worthy of consideration include 
levetiracetam, lamotrigine, and zonisamide. The tricyclic antide -
pressant class may also be used to treat neuropathic pain; again, 
the side effects (dry mouth, constipation, drowsiness, urinary  
retention, sexual dysfunction) may pose a challenge for patients.
Patients who are unable to tolerate the side effects or obtain 
clinically meaningful relief from oral medications may consider 
intrathecal therapy via an implanted drug delivery device. Morphine 
and ziconotide are both approved for the management of neuro -
pathic pain via intrathecal infusion. One small study demon -
strated the safety and effectiveness of intrathecal morphine and 
baclofen for the treatment of spasticity and neuropathic pain. 169 
Ziconotide is a nonopioid agent that has demonstrated significant 
pain reduction over placebo. 115,161 It selectively targets voltage-
gated calcium channels, prevents the primary afferent transmis -
sion of pain signals in the spinal cord, and is a viable option for 
patients who do not respond to opioids and has the additional 
benefit of not causing tolerance, dependence, or respiratory  
depression.62 Extremely slow titration is still advised to prevent 
side effects.115 Most PwMS have limited tolerance for medications 
that may cause fatigue; therefore pump delivery is an appealing 
alternative to oral/systemic agents and may provide pain relief 
without the burdensome effects of sedation or confusion.
Patients may also complain of pain at injection sites associated 
with DMTs. A large-scale review found that subcutaneous drug 
formulations have increased injection site reactions compared 
with intramuscular injections. 9 Patient- and clinician-reported 
injection site reactions include the presence of localized burning, 
tenderness, swelling, or necrosis. Suggested techniques to reduce 
injection site reactions include use of an autoinjector, topical  
anesthetic agents, massage, and cooling packs. This issue is less 
concerning given the greater number of DMT formulations now 
available.
T rigeminal neuralgia (sudden unilateral pain in the distribu -
tion of the fifth cranial nerve) affects approximately 2% to 6% of 
patients with MS and is 20 times more common in PwMS than 
in the general population.109 It may be a result of focal compres -
sion of the trigeminal nerve root near its entry at the pons (pri -
mary trigeminal neuralgia); it can also stem from a neurologic 
diagnosis, such as demyelinating plaques in the same region or  
a posterior fossa tumor (symptomatic trigeminal neuralgia).  
Patients complain of spontaneous, unilateral, lancinating “electri-
cal sparks.” Light, tactile sensation, or pressure may stimulate the 
pain, which can last for seconds to hours. Diagnosis may be chal-
lenging because the pain may be referred from any of the three 
branches of the trigeminal nerve, and patients may misconstrue 
their symptoms for dental pain. Blink reflex studies have reported 
delays of the R1 response on the symptomatic side. 49 There is no 
standardized MRI sequence to screen for neurovascular compres -
sion. T reatment options for trigeminal neuralgia include anticon-
vulsants (carbamazepine, gabapentin, lamotrigine, topiramate), 
antispasmodics (baclofen), and, in rare instances, narcotics. Again, 
most PwMS are hesitant to take opiates because of the cognitive 
and physical clouding that may occur. Misoprostol, a prostaglan -
din E analog, also relieved pain in patients with trigeminal neu -
ralgia and should be considered. 55 Misoprostol is believed to be 
effective because the T-cell inhibition caused by prostaglandins 
leads to an antiinflammatory effect in plaques. Doses of misopro-
stol ranged from 100 to 200 m g titrated up to four times a day. 
Patients who are refractory to or unable to tolerate oral medica -
tions may consider BoNT injections or neurosurgery referral for 
microvascular decompression, rhizotomy, or Gamma Knife repair.
Some other MS symptoms mentioned earlier that the clinician 
should be aware of include pain associated with optic neuritis,  
a Lhermitte sign, and the “MS hug.” Inflammation of the optic 
nerve causes optic neuritis. It is often painful and worse with move-
ment; photosensitivity and/or blurred vision may also be present.

The symptom is sometimes self-limiting but may also require treat-
ment with steroids (intravenous methylprednisolone) or carbam -
azepine. A Lhermitte sign is an electrical sensation that occurs down 
the length of the spine and is exacerbated with neck flexion. Some

“MS hug” is described by patients as a feeling of tightness across  
the ribs, as if they were being hugged very tightly. Burning and 
tingling sensations are common. T reatment with anticonvulsants  
or topical compound mixtures of anticonvulsants and lidocaine  
is suggested. The causes of the Lhermitte sign and the “MS hug”

Neurogenic Lower Urinary Tract  
Dysfunction, Neurogenic Bowel  
Dysfunction, and Sexual Dysfunction
Patients with MS are highly disposed to bladder, bowel, and 
sexual dysfunction. The following three sections focus on areas 
specific for MS. The reader is advised to consult Chapters 21, 22, 
and 23 for further detail on each area.
Neurogenic Lower Urinary Tract Dysfunction
The presence of lesions in the brain and/or spinal cord may lead 
to disorders of emptying (double voiding, incomplete emptying, 
slow or intermittent stream), storage (urinary urgency, frequency, 
nocturia, incontinence), or a mixture of both (detrusor sphincter 
dyssynergia). It affects up to 75% of PwMS.135 Many studies have 
corroborated the presence of increased urinary dysfunction in 
patients with MS when plaques are present in the spinal cord. 1 
Patients may isolate themselves socially because of the unpredict -
able nature of the incontinence. The presence of gait dysfunction 
is correlated with the presence of neurogenic lower urinary  
tract dysfunction (NLUTD).61,78 Guidelines for the screening of 
NLUTD include an appropriate history and evaluation of the 
postvoid residual. The need and utility of urodynamic evaluation 
remain controversial. Early screening is advised to prevent long-
term complications in the urinary tract.
T reatment includes pelvic muscle training, medication, BoNT 
injection, and surgery. Pharmacologic treatment consists of  
antimuscarinic agents for treating disorders of storage (urgency/
incontinence). Mirabegron, a beta agonist, has been shown to 
improve urgency, frequency, and incontinence.211 Alpha antago -
nists are commonly recommended for the treatment of emptying 
disorders because they relax the smooth muscle of the bladder 
neck. Desmopressin may be indicated for symptoms of nocturia 
because it enhances water reabsorption to decrease urine volume. 
Appropriate screening for the side effects of medications (constipa-
tion, dry mouth, fatigue, cognitive slowing) and electrolyte distur-
bance (hyponatremia) is advised.
BoNT type A (BoNT-A), specifically onabotulinum toxin, is 
US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved for the treat-
ment of neurogenic detrusor overactivity in PwMS. Intravesical 
injections of BoNT-A have been found to increase the bladder 
capacity and reduce episodes of urinary incontinence by prevent -
ing the release of acetylcholine, causing the bladder wall to relax. 
Adverse effects include urinary tract infection and urinary reten -
tion. Patients should be advised of the possible need to catheterize 
postinjection if postvoid residuals are elevated. The benefits have 
been reported to last up to 8 to 10 months, although injections 
may be performed every 6 months. BoNT effectiveness may wear 
off over time with repetitive use; however, multiple randomized, 
double-blind, placebo-controlled trials have shown sustained  
improvement in incontinence and quality of life. 142 The clinician 
should be aware of the specific serotype of BoNT to be used and 
that other toxins may not be interchanged or interconverted.105,112 
Given the ever-expanding role of BoNT , the clinician is also  
advised to ask the patient about other exposure to the toxin.  
In instances where BoNT injections are occurring in the same 
patient for multiple indications, coordination of care between 
disciplines is essential. Nerve stimulation has also been evaluated 
for the treatment of NLUTD. Posterior tibial nerve stimulation 
involves needle insertion superior and medial to the medial mal -
leolus with stimulation sufficient to induce toe flexion and tin -
gling distal to and including the ankle. Its exact mechanism of 
action is unknown. The procedure is indicated in patients who 
have failed or are unable to tolerate conservative treatment for 
overactive bladder and typically involves 12 weekly 30-minute 
sessions. The use of posterior tibial nerve stimulation has demon-
strated a clear reduction in urinary incontinence, urgency, fre -
quency, and number of incontinence pads used. 104 Long-term 
effectiveness up to 12 months has been reported. 126
Sacral nerve stimulation is another option for the treatment of 
hyperreflexia, urge incontinence, and urgency/frequency disor -
ders. Its exact mechanism of action is unknown, but it is believed 
to restore the balance between excitatory and inhibitory stimula -
tion of the sacral and suprasacral regions. 13 A trial period is  
advised for 3 to 7 days, with a pretrial and posttrial voiding diary 
evaluation for effectiveness. 51 Sacral nerve stimulation requires a 
surgical procedure, and its use is limited because of lack of MRI 
compatibility of the devices. Currently, some devices are contra -
indicated for use of MRI, whereas others permit brain MRI only.
Neurogenic Bowel Dysfunction
Neurogenic bowel dysfunction presents as constipation or fecal 
incontinence. PwMS have reported spending more than 30 min -
utes per day on their bowel regimen. Constipation is believed to be 
caused by immobilization or abnormal colonic contractility, tone, 
or rectoanal hypercompliance.8 Medication side effects must also 
be considered. Patients with overactive bladders may limit their 
fluid intake so as to prevent incontinence, which may further con-
tribute to constipation. Noninvasive procedures should be consid-
ered first, including timing with gastrocolic reflex, positioning to 
use gravity to the patient’s advantage, and patient-specific laxative 
regimens.83 The combination of senna and bulk laxatives (i.e., 
psyllium) has been shown to be more effective than lactulose. Fecal 
incontinence may be attributable to loss of control of the external 
anal sphincter, abnormal rectosigmoid compliance, decreased  
sensation of rectal filling, or weakness of the anal sphincter. The 
use of probiotics can help both constipation and incontinence. 
Biofeedback can also help PwMS become more conscious of 
stimulus in the rectal area and teach them to relax the anal sphinc-
ter. Referral to gastroenterology may be needed to evaluate if other 
underlying medical issues may be causing the incontinence.
Sexual Dysfunction
Sexual dysfunction is present in approximately 42% to 90% of 
PwMS.34,128 Despite the high prevalence, only 20% of patients 
report being asked about sexual dysfunction during clinical visits. 
Females commonly report decreased libido and loss of lubrica -
tion, and males report erectile and ejaculatory dysfunction. Both 
sexes report challenges with achieving orgasm. Side effects of 
DMT and their treatment (antidepressants, antispasmodics, alpha 
antagonists) and fatigue should be evaluated. Primary sexual

impairs sexual feelings and/or the response. This includes symp -
toms of decreased libido or its loss as well as paresthesias that 
make the sexual encounter painful or unpleasurable. Secondary 
sexual dysfunction is attributable to disability secondary to MS 
and includes items that indirectly affect the sexual response, such 
as NLUTD or neurogenic bowel dysfunction symptoms, spastic -
ity, tremor, or immobility. Medical conditions unrelated to MS, 
such as mood disorders, hypertension, and diabetes, are also in -
cluded in secondary sexual dysfunction. T ertiary sexual dysfunc -
tion involves one’s view of self and the culture’s view of sex, 
physical intimacy, and their importance. Family dynamics, inter-
personal conflict, poor self-esteem, and altered views of one’s body 
image can all be considered causes of tertiary sexual dysfunction.
Screening for sexual dysfunction may be done during the  
intake history or examination. The MS Intimacy and Sexuality 
Questionnaire is a 19-item patient self-report questionnaire that 
asks about sexual activity, satisfaction, and presence of symptoms 
or other issues that may interfere with sexual encounters.
173 It can 
also help the clinician to determine if the problem is attributable 
to primary, secondary, or tertiary causes. The scale has demon -
strated validity in terms of marital satisfaction, including com-
munication, problem solving, sexual dissatisfaction, and level and 
extent of neurologic impairment as a result of MS, psychological 
well-being, and global sexual dysfunction in MS.
Clinicians are encouraged to ask PwMS about and discuss this 
issue. Patients may not be aware of the high prevalence of sexual 
dysfunction in MS and may feel that they are to blame. Reducing 
use of medications that have side effects of impairing libido can 
be helpful, as well as initiating medications that target spasms or 
pain. According to a recent meta review, first-line treatment  
includes physical exercises such as yoga and pelvic floor exercises, 
followed by assistive devices and/or pharmacologic treatment.
73 
Sildenafil citrate is known to be effective for male patients with 
erectile dysfunction.
64 Females with sexual dysfunction were tri -
aled on sildenafil citrate for improvement in sexual response, 
blood flow, and neurophysiologic response. Although lubrication 
improved, no change was noted in quality of life or in tibial and 
pudendal nerve evoked studies.
52 BoNT-A received via intradetru-
sorial injections also improved sexual function in 70% of female 
patients in a small study.
73 Psychological counseling for the  
patient and/or the couple may be helpful. In addition, referral to 
a neuropsychologist is advised for additional evaluation and man-
agement options.
Cognitive Impairment
Cognitive impairment may be present at any stage of MS.  
Approximately 40% to 70% of PwMS have cognitive impair -
ment, which in PwMS include episodic memory loss, attention 
deficits, delayed processing speed, and difficulty in executive func-
tion. Dementia is less commonly present in MS (Fig. 47.6).
133 
Although neuropsychological testing remains the most sensitive 
and comprehensive assessment tool for cognitive impairment, the 
expense and time involved for testing remain formidable barriers. 
In addition, PwMS are often affected by fatigue, making extensive 
testing even more challenging. Other tests to evaluate and assess 
cognitive function have been studied. The Rao Brief Repeatable 
Battery of Neuropsychological T ests and the Minimal Assessment 
of Cognitive Function in MS both assess working memory,  
processing speed, new learning, and language.

assess processing speed, one of the most common challenges in 
patients with long-standing MS.
Various structural factors on MRI have correlated with cogni -
tive impairments in PwMS. Increased cortical atrophy, widening 
of the third ventricle, and overall loss of brain volume are more 
often seen in persons with cognitive impairment.
19,36 Further  
research into the significance of gray matter changes and cognitive 
impairment is underway. Longitudinal studies have demonstrated 
early brain atrophy as a predictor for future cognitive impairment.
One year of natalizumab treatment demonstrated significant 
improvement in cognitive performance and fatigue in patients 
with RRMS.
102 Many patients also benefit from the use of  
psychostimulants, such as methylphenidate and levoamphet -
amine, to improve attention, learning, and the ability to focus.18,88  
Memantine and donepezil have been tested, with conflicting  
results, for the treatment of memory disorders. 91 A meta-analysis 
showed evidence was inadequate for the efficacy of memantine on 
cognitive impairment.
199 The supplement Gingko biloba did not 
improve cognitive performance in PwMS.124
Factors that may affect cognitive impairment include disease 
duration, MS subtype, level of disability, and premorbid level of 
verbal competence. One striking feature is the concomitant pres -
ence of depression or depressive symptoms in patients with cogni-
tive impairment, further emphasizing the need for vigorous 
screening for mood disorders in this patient population.
T wo hypotheses prevail in what determines the extent of cog-
nitive dysfunction and why individuals are affected differently. 
Brain reserve or maximal lifetime brain volume (MLBV) is deter-
mined primarily by genetics.
11,191 Those individuals with larger 
MLBVs are believed to be less susceptible to cognitive decline and 
have a lower risk of dementia. The cognitive reserve hypothesis 
states that learning is acquired from life experiences, education, 
and cognitive leisure activities (such as reading, writing, doing 
puzzles, or playing board games). A positive correlation was found 
between cognition and both education and early life cognitive
