both protective against cognitive impairment.
In addition to DMT , various rehabilitative interventions have 
been used for prevention and the treatment of cognitive impair -
ment in PwMS. Cognitive behavioral therapy has reduced distress 
and improved cognitive performance in several small studies.137,189 
Cognitive training has also been trialed, with approaches often 
relying on repetitive training for targeted cognitive functions such 
as processing speed or working memory, often completed on a 
computer program with moderate effect on cognitive impair -
ment.20 Long-term benefit is unknown.
Swallowing
Dysphagia, or difficulty with swallowing liquids or solids, may 
present at any stage of MS. An estimated 33% to 43% of patients 
are affected.184 The prevalence of dysphagia increases with increas-
ing disability, EDSS scores, and disease duration.22,31 The etiology 
of dysphagia in MS is likely to be attributable to the involvement 
of corticobulbar, cerebellar, or brainstem regions.
A thorough history is crucial when a patient is being screened 
for dysphagia and can assist in localizing what phase of swallow -
ing is affected. The pharyngeal phase is most frequently affected 
in MS. The presence of xerostomia (dry mouth) or globus (non -
painful sensation of a lump or fullness in the throat) should be 
excluded.46 Many medications used for MS symptoms can cause 
xerostomia. The presence of another unrelated comorbidity may 
also be present, causing difficulty with swallowing. Patients may 
complete the 10-item self-report called the DYMUS (Screening 
with Dysphagia in MS) questionnaire to assess their risk for dys -
phagia. This has been validated to screen for dysphagia of both 
solids and liquids. It may serve as a screening tool to assess the 
need for additional testing as well as to identify patients at risk for 
aspiration pneumonia.22,23 Clinical assessment alone may under -
estimate the risk of aspiration by 50%. 46 A video radiographic 
swallow study, commonly called the modified barium swallow, is 
the tool of choice for evaluating the presence of silent aspiration. 
Patients may require referral for additional testing, including 
esophageal manometry or an endoscopy.
T reating dysphagia with compensatory strategies is often  
effective. This includes (1) postural changes, (2) modifying the 
size of the food bolus consumed, and (3) changing the food’s 
consistency.31 Other treatments include electrical stimulation 
and neurotoxin injection. Pharyngeal electrical stimulation to 
treat oropharyngeal dysphagia significantly reduces the amount 
of penetration and aspiration. 162 Injection of BoNT into the 
cricopharyngeal muscle has been reported to be effective for the 
treatment of upper esophageal hyperactivity. 163
Pseudobulbar Affect
PBA is a disorder of uncontrollable laughter or crying that is incon-
gruent with the social or professional situation.69,134 Although PBA 
can be seen in almost any illness, this is commonly seen in persons 
with neurologic disorders, including MS, amyotrophic lateral scle-
rosis (ALS), stroke, and traumatic brain injury. The prevalence in 
MS is believed to be 6.5% to 46.26%.132 The emotional lability in 
PBA is spontaneous and brief. Patients who suffer from this may 
feel socially isolated because of their lack of control over such laugh-
ing or crying episodes. In PBA, affect is not congruent with mood 
(patient experience crying while feeling happy), whereas in depres-
sion, affect matches mood. The duration of symptoms is brief in 
PBA, whereas a depressed mood typically lasts weeks to months. 
PBA constitutes a true disorder and should be differentiated from 
pseudobulbar syndrome, in which corticobulbar damage may lead 
to oromotor dysfunction, dysphonia, and slow slurred speech.
The pathophysiology is not clear, but PBA is believed to be a 
disorder of the cortico-pontine-cerebellar circuit, which descends 
to the basilar part of the pons, brainstem, and cerebellum.71,134,147 
It is thought that disruption here can lower the threshold for 
emotional response147–149 and thus lead to an emotional incon -
gruence ( Fig. 47.7 ). The Center for Neurologic Study–Lability 
Scale (CNS-LS) is a seven-item self-reported scale to screen for 
PBA. It has been validated in both MS and ALS. Although a score 
of 13 or more on the CNS-LS is indicative of PBA, one study 
noted a sensitivity of 94% and a specificity of 83% with a score 
of 17 or more in PwMS. 136,182 This screening tool is quick and 
efficient and can be used to track a patient once treatment  
has begun.
Various agents, including SSRIs and tricyclic antidepres -
sants, have been used with varying success to treat PBA. These 
studies had small numbers of patients and varied in methodology  
(Table 47.6). The combination of dextromethorphan with low-
dose quinidine has resulted in a successful treatment for patients 
with PBA. Its mechanism of action is unknown. A double-blind, 
placebo-controlled study using dextromethorphan/low-dose 
quinidine in patients with ALS and MS resulted in a significantly 
reduced rate of pseudobulbar episodes per day compared with 
placebo (49% reduction, P , .0001).150 Participants in the active 
arm noticed improvement as soon as 1 week into the study. The 
most common adverse events included headache, falls, diarrhea, 
and vertigo. Dextromethorphan/quinidine may cause QTc pro -
longation. The recommended dosage is 1 capsule daily for 7 days 
followed by 1 capsule every 12 hours beginning on day 8.  
Most patients noticed a reduction in lability after 1 to 2 weeks of 
treatment.
Evidence for Rehabilitation in  
Multiple Sclerosis
In a recent Cochrane review comprising 164 randomized con -
trolled trials and four controlled clinical trials with 10,396 subjects, 
moderate-quality evidence suggested that physical therapeutic  
modalities (exercise and physical activities) improved functional 
outcomes (mobility, muscular strength), reduced impairment  
(fatigue), and improved participation (quality of life).2 Moderate-
quality evidence suggested that inpatient or outpatient multidisci-
plinary rehabilitation programs led to longer term gains at the  
levels of activity and participation, and interventions that provided  
information improved patient knowledge. Low-quality evidence 
suggested that neuropsychological interventions, symptom man -
agement programs (especially for spasticity), whole-body vibration, 
and telerehabilitation improved some patient outcomes. Evidence 
for other rehabilitation modalities was inconclusive in this review 
because of lack of quality research studies.
Pediatric Multiple Sclerosis
Our understanding of demyelinating disorders in children has 
increased in recent years. According to the International Pediatric 
MS Study Group, pediatric MS can be diagnosed after two clini -
cal episodes of CNS demyelination that are separated by at least 
30 days. Pediatric MS accounts for 5% of all MS cases. It provides

In forming a differential diagnosis, monophasic demyelinating

a first demyelinating episode of pediatric MS. ADEM is defined  
as a first clinical event affecting the CNS—an event of inflamma -

involving a polysymptomatic presentation.
210 Encephalopathy  
can be defined as behavioral change with confusion, excessive  
irritability, or alteration of consciousness. Interestingly, data sug-
gest that encephalopathy can be associated with a first episode of 
MS, especially in younger patients. Rather than being specific for 
a disease process, it may be an indication of an immature brain or 
immune system.
Children tend to have a larger lesion burden, particularly with 
brainstem and cerebellar involvement.

Multiple Sclerosis in Pregnancy
There is a strong female predominance in MS. Patients are often 
diagnosed in their child-bearing years. As such, pregnancy  
becomes an important topic for female patients with MS at 
some point during their disease course. At least 20% to 30%  
of patients will have children after their diagnosis of MS.
47  
Fortunately, MS has little to no direct effect on pregnancy.  
Patients can be counseled with confidence that their disease will

not affect their ability to conceive, the safety and health of the 
pregnancy, or fetal well-being. There is no increased risk for 
spontaneous abortion, ectopic pregnancy, cesarean delivery, or 
major neonatal or obstetric complications. It is well known that 
pregnancy is a time of stability for patients with MS. Disease 
activity is markedly reduced during the third trimester. Preg-
nancy is considered an immune-tolerant state in which the  
maternal immune system adapts to an allogeneic pregnancy. 
Several biologic changes occur during pregnancy that may affect 
MS. Hormone levels increase during pregnancy—including  
estrogens (especially estriol), progesterone, prolactin, and gluco -
corticoids—and then fall dramatically during the postpartum 
period. These hormones affect the immune system and help to 
shift cytokines, decrease adhesion molecules and matrix metal-
loproteinases, decrease antigen presentation, and boost T reg 
cells.
47 This results in an overall decrease in inflammatory pro -
cesses. There are also significant immune cell shifts that involve 
T reg cells, Th17 cells, and natural killer cells. Fetal antigens  
interact with and modulate the maternal immune system.  
Peripheral T reg cells appear that are specific for fetal antigens. 
Pregnancy also may result in positive benefits to the CNS  
by promoting endogenous recovery mechanisms and enhancing 
the ability to respond to immune-mediated injury.
There is concern about medication use during pregnancy.  
For patients with MS, medication concerns can be related to 
DMTs, treatments for relapses, or management of symptoms. 
Ideally, DMTs should not be used in patients with MS who are 
pregnant or trying to become pregnant. However, in patients 
with features associated with poor prognosis (as mentioned ear -
lier) there is a heightened risk of relapse even during pregnancy, 
and this can negatively impact both the pregnancy and long-term 
disability accumulation. In these patients, ocrelizumab has 
emerged as a popular treatment option because of its high effi-
cacy, favorable 1-month half-life, convenience of dosing (which 
allows planning of conception 1–2 months after an infusion), 
and relative safety in pregnancy. Glatiramer acetate has the most 
favorable safety rating (class B) in pregnancy and is still being 
used in certain centers, although this approach is gradually fall -
ing out of favor. Acute relapses can be treated with a short course 
of high-dose glucocorticoids as determined by the patient’s prac-
titioner. If possible, it is preferable to forego treatment during  
the first trimester because of the association of cleft palate and 
lower birth rate. However, corticosteroids are commonly used in 
the second and third trimesters. Prednisone, prednisolone, and 
methylprednisolone are preferred because they are metabolized 
to inactive forms by the placenta, rather than betamethasone  
and dexamethasone, which cross the placenta with minimal  
metabolism. As a general principle, medications for the treat-
ment of symptoms are minimized during pregnancy. However,  
if a medication is needed to prevent suffering or maintain quality 
of life, it can be continued. Compared with pre-pregnancy,  
annualized relapse rates fall by 70% during the third trimester. 
During the 3 months postpartum, the relapse rate rebounds to 
70% above the prepregnancy level and then comes down and 
stays down at the prepregnancy rate. The most consistent marker 
associated with postpartum activity is the disease activity in the

Summary
The past decade has seen more than a doubling of treatment  
options for patients with MS. One of the biggest and most impact-
ful changes with regard to medication compliance and ease has 
been the approval of oral DMTs. Diagnoses of MS occur earlier 
and with improved accuracy. Patients are no longer satisfied with 
“not getting worse.” They want and demand options that will  
increase their strength and endurance and maintain their ability  
to ambulate; in essence, they want us as clinicians to help them 
enhance their quality of life. The varying nature of MS symptoms 
presents unique challenges to the clinician. The disease is progres-
sive; symptoms change often and are affected by external factors, 
including heat, fatigue, and side effects of DMTs. As physiatrists, 
we hold the answers and options for this in our treatment tool 
bags. We have therapeutic and symptomatic options for fitness and 
symptom management, and we can effectively educate and guide 
these patients. In addition, our training and expertise can help 
these patients develop coping strategies so that they can manage 
the physical, mental, and/or cognitive challenges they may face.
